1: τῇ μιᾷ τῶν σαββάτων -- "on the first day of the week" -- literally "on the one of the Sabbaths." The unusual Greek phrasing reflects the underlying Semitic idiom for day-count within the week. The "first day" becomes the day of resurrection; its recurrence in 20:19 and 26 marks it as the day of gathering -- the eventual pattern of Christian worship. σκοτίας ἔτι οὔσης -- "while it was still dark" -- the darkness is temporal but the Evangelist has made darkness a theological marker throughout the Gospel (John 1:5; John 8:12; John 13:30); its presence at the resurrection's beginning gives way to the discovery of the empty tomb.
2: ἦραν τὸν κύριον -- "they have taken the Lord." Mary's assumption is grave robbery. The plural oidamen (we do not know) suggests she was not alone -- consistent with the Synoptic accounts -- though the Evangelist focuses on her. The "other disciple" (ton allon mathētēn) is introduced here for the first time in the resurrection narrative; the Evangelist maintains his pattern of referring to the Beloved Disciple by this circumlocution.
3: Brief connector. The two disciples set out together; their running to the tomb (John 20:3-8) is narrated with unusual physical detail -- arrival order, entry order, objects seen -- suggesting an eyewitness source.
4: προέδραμεν τάχιον τοῦ Πέτρου -- "outran Peter" -- the comparative tachion (more quickly) is precise and vivid. The detail serves no obvious theological purpose, which is itself an argument for its historical basis. The Beloved Disciple arrives first but does not enter; Peter enters immediately upon arrival (John 20:6).
5: παρακύψας βλέπει κείμενα τὰ ὀθόνια -- "stooping down, sees the linen cloths lying." The verb blepei is the ordinary verb for visual perception; it contrasts with theōrei (observe/contemplate) used for Peter in John 20:6 and eiden (saw with understanding) for the Beloved Disciple in John 20:8. The Evangelist has a consistent pattern of using different seeing verbs for different depths of perception.
6: θεωρεῖ τὰ ὀθόνια κείμενα -- Peter uses theōrei -- to observe, contemplate, take in. He sees the linen cloths and (John 20:7) the face-cloth separately arranged. The Evangelist does not record that Peter believed at this point; John 20:8 reserves that response for the Beloved Disciple. Peter's reaction is unnarrated -- left open.
7: τὸ σουδάριον -- the napkin or face-cloth -- found not with the othonia (body wrappings) but separately, rolled up in its own place. This detail is central to the narrative's argument against grave robbery: a thief would not have taken the time to unwrap the body and arrange the cloths. The orderliness of the scene signals deliberate action. The word entetyligmenon (wrapped together, folded) carries the sense of a completed action left in a specific state.
8: εἶδεν καὶ ἐπίστευσεν -- "saw and believed." The verb eiden here (G3708 root) implies perception that grasps significance. This is the Evangelist's description of the Beloved Disciple's Easter faith: he saw the empty tomb with its arranged grave-clothes and believed -- without a resurrection appearance, without fulfillment-Scripture citation. The parenthetical John 20:9 explains that the two disciples did not yet understand the resurrection from Scripture; the Beloved Disciple believed on physical evidence alone.
9: οὐδέπω γὰρ ᾔδεισαν τὴν γραφήν -- "for they did not yet know the Scripture." The Evangelist inserts a parenthetical editorial note -- his characteristic device (cf. John 2:21; John 7:39; John 12:16). The pluperfect ēideisan indicates that scriptural understanding came later; the disciples' Easter faith preceded and was independent of their later biblical interpretation. Faith can precede full theological understanding.
10: Brief connector. The disciples return home. The narrative turns entirely to Mary's encounter, narrated in greater detail than either disciple's experience.
11: εἱστήκει δὲ Μαριὰμ πρὸς τῷ μνημείῳ ἔξω κλαίουσα -- "Mary stood outside the tomb weeping." The pluperfect heistēkei marks her sustained, still posture against the disciples' departure. The verb klaiein (weep, wail) is the same word used for the community's weeping at Lazarus's tomb (John 11:31-33), where Jesus himself wept. The parallelism is deliberate: in John 11 Jesus raised another; here the women and disciples mourn Jesus himself.
12: δύο ἀγγέλους ἐν λευκοῖς -- "two angels in white, sitting where the body of Jesus had lain." The Evangelist notes the positional detail -- one at the head, one at the feet -- of the divine messengers. The angels' role in John is minimal: they do not announce the resurrection but ask Mary a question, drawing out her response.
13: ἦραν τὸν κύριόν μου -- "they have taken away my Lord." Mary's personal possessive (mou) is striking -- language of personal relationship, not merely formal title. The Evangelist's consistent use of kurios (Lord) for the risen Jesus throughout John 20 is a post-resurrection Christological datum; Mary's adoption of the title here is his earliest instance of it in the resurrection appearances.
14: ἐστράφη εἰς τὰ ὀπίσω -- "turned around" -- Mary turns away from the angels to see Jesus standing behind her. The reason she does not recognize him (ouk ēidei hoti Iēsous estin) is unexplained. Non-recognition is a pattern in the resurrection appearances (John 20:14; Luke 24:16). The narrative focus is on the moment of recognition (John 20:16), not on explaining its absence.
15: κηπουρός -- "gardener" (kēpouros), a unique NT hapax legomenon. The misidentification is dramatic irony: the reader knows who Jesus is. The Evangelist may also register the Eden resonance (cf. John 19:41: the tomb is in a garden), though secondary to the simple narrative of non-recognition. Mary's offer to carry the body herself captures both her devotion and the impossibility of what she proposes.
16: Μαριάμ -- Jesus speaks her name, and in the address she recognizes him. The Evangelist reports her response in Aramaic -- Rabbounī (Rabbouni), then provides the Greek gloss "which means Teacher." The Aramaic address is striking: this is the language of intimacy, the mother-tongue address. The scene echoes John 10:3 ("the shepherd calls his own sheep by name"); the sheep know his voice.
17:
μή μου ἅπτου -- "stop holding onto me" -- the present imperative with mē stops an ongoing action: Mary has already grasped him. The verb haptesthai is not "touch" (which would be an aorist prohibition) but "cling to, hold." The reason given -- "for I have not yet ascended to the Father" -- implies the current state is transitional. The Evangelist offers no resolution of the theological difficulty.

ἀναβαίνω -- the present tense ("I am ascending") -- not future. The resurrection and ascension are presented as a unified movement in the Fourth Gospel, not separated by forty days as in Acts 1. The message to "my brothers" (tous adelphous mou) is the first use of this fraternal language for the disciples in John.
18: ἑώρακα τὸν κύριον -- "I have seen the Lord" -- perfect tense: a completed past act with present abiding significance. Mary becomes the first resurrection witness in John's account. The Evangelist notes that she reported what Jesus had said -- underscoring that her testimony included both the appearance and the words.
19: εἰρήνη ὑμῖν -- "Peace to you" -- the standard Jewish greeting shalom / eirēnē, but freighted now with the content of the farewell discourses (John 14:27; John 16:33). The greeting is given with doors locked -- tōn thurōn kekleismenōn (perfect participle, standing shut). Jesus's appearance does not depend on physical access; the Evangelist does not explain the mechanics, only the fact.
20: ἔδειξεν τὰς χεῖρας καὶ τὴν πλευράν -- "showed them his hands and his side." The wounds are part of the identification -- the same body that was crucified is the body that is risen. The disciples' response (echarēsan, rejoiced) fulfills John 16:22: "your hearts will rejoice and no one will take your joy from you." The Evangelist marks the fulfillment with quiet precision.
21: καθὼς ἀπέσταλκέν με ὁ πατήρ, κἀγὼ πέμπω ὑμᾶς -- "As the Father has sent me, so I send you." Two different verbs for "send": apostellō (Father's act, with its official, commissioned force) and pempō (Jesus's act, the more general sending). Whether the distinction is significant here is debated; the Evangelist uses both verbs for both Father and Son elsewhere. The mission of the disciples is explicitly modeled on and derived from the mission of the Son.
22: ἐνεφύσησεν -- "breathed on them" -- a rare verb (emphusaō). The same word appears in the LXX of Genesis 2:7, where God breathes into the nostrils of the man and he becomes a living being. The Evangelist's use of this word for Jesus's act is not accidental: the new creation parallels the first creation. The command Labete Pneuma Hagion -- "Receive Holy Spirit" (anarthrous, no definite article) -- is an aorist imperative. This is not the same event as Pentecost (Acts 2); the Evangelist presents a distinct bestowing. Harmonization with Acts is possible but the two accounts are not identical.
23: ἄν τινων ἀφῆτε τὰς ἁμαρτίας -- "whoever's sins you forgive, they are forgiven." The authority communicated here is corporate and verbal -- the disciples are entrusted with the ministry of forgiveness and its withholding (kratēte, retain). The verbs are present tense in the consequence clause: forgiveness and retention are ongoing realities of the community's ministry. This commission is connected to the Holy Spirit's gift in John 20:22 -- the Spirit enables and authorizes the ministry.
24: Θωμᾶς ὁ λεγόμενος Δίδυμος -- Thomas called Didymus (Twin in Aramaic and Greek respectively). The Evangelist introduces him with his dual-name designation (as in John 11:16; John 21:2) and notes his absence without explanation. His absence is narrative setup for the scene in John 20:25-28; its cause is not supplied.
25: ἐὰν μὴ ἴδω … οὐ μὴ πιστεύσω -- "unless I see... I will not believe" -- a strong double negative (ou mē) with subjunctive: the most emphatic negation in Greek. Thomas's demand for tangible evidence is often called skepticism, but the Evangelist presents it as the same desire all the disciples had already been given (John 20:20: Jesus showed them his hands and side). Thomas wants what the other disciples received. His demand is for consistency of evidence, not excess of doubt.
26: μεθ᾽ ἡμέρας ὀκτώ -- "after eight days" -- one week later. The repeated "first day of the week" gathering (cf. John 20:19) is the beginning of what will become the pattern of the Lord's Day. Jesus again appears through locked doors with the same greeting -- Eirēnē hymin -- establishing a structural parallel. Thomas is now present.
27: Φέρε τὸν δάκτυλόν σου ὧδε -- "Bring your finger here." Jesus addresses Thomas using the exact language of Thomas's demand (John 20:25): the nail marks, the side. The aorist imperatives Phere (bring!) and Ide (see!) are commands, not invitations. μὴ γίνου ἄπιστος ἀλλὰ πιστός -- "do not be faithless but faithful" -- the present imperative ginou commands a becoming, not merely a momentary act. Jesus's intervention is a summons to a state of faith.
28:
ὁ Κύριός μου καὶ ὁ Θεός μου -- "My Lord and my God!" This is the highest explicit Christological confession in the Gospel. Thomas does not merely address Jesus as Lord but adds ho Theos mou -- my God. The double article with the personal pronoun gives the affirmation its full confessional weight. Whether Thomas actually touches the wounds is not stated -- the Evangelist leaves this ambiguous.

The Evangelist has structured the Gospel so that it opens with a Christological declaration about God (John 1:1: the Logos was God) and closes with a human character making the same confession. The inclusion is deliberate.
29: ὅτι ἑώρακάς με πεπίστευκας -- "because you have seen me you have believed" -- perfect tenses for both verbs: the seeing is a completed act, the believing a continuing state. The beatitude that follows -- makarioi hoi mē idontes kai pisteusantes -- "blessed those who have not seen and yet have believed" -- is addressed beyond the narrative to the Evangelist's readers, who cannot see the risen Jesus. It is the Gospel's explicit gesture toward its audience.
30: πολλὰ μὲν οὖν καὶ ἄλλα σημεῖα -- "many other signs also." The Evangelist explicitly acknowledges selectivity. He has chosen the signs he has recorded; others exist that are not written. The word sēmeia (signs) is the Evangelist's characteristic term for Jesus's works -- pointing beyond themselves to something about the one who performs them.
31:
ταῦτα δὲ γέγραπται ἵνα πιστεύ[σ]ητε -- "these are written so that you may believe." The Evangelist states his purpose in writing. The verb has two major manuscript variants: pisteusēte (aorist subjunctive: come to believe, initial faith) and pisteūēte (present subjunctive: continue believing, sustained faith). Both are well-attested. The aorist reading supports a mission to outsiders; the present supports an address to believers. The Gospel serves both; the ambiguity may be intentional.

ζωὴν ἔχητε ἐν τῷ ὀνόματι αὐτοῦ -- life in his name. The goal of belief is life; the means is the name -- the revealed character of Jesus as Messiah and Son of God. The Gospel ends on the term that opened it: life (zōē) in 1:4 was in the Logos; in 20:31 it is the goal of writing the Gospel.
